name: Naturepedia Soil Carbon Systems Knowledge Mesh
description: Machine-readable semantic knowledge mesh connecting Soil Carbon Systems™ with related Naturepedia systems.
architecture: Carbon Cycle™ → Soil Systems™ → Soil Carbon Systems™ → Root Carbon Pathways → Mycorrhizal Carbon → Microbial Carbon Pump → Soil Aggregates → Carbon Stabilization → Ecosystem Feedbacks™ → Climate Carbon Feedbacks™

relationshipMesh.relationship: The carbon cycle supplies organic carbon that enters soils through plant residues, roots, litter, microbial biomass, fungal networks, decomposition, and long-term stabilization.
Soil systems create and protect soil organic matter through biological activity, mineral interactions, aggregation, and moisture regulation.
Plants allocate carbon belowground through root exudates, rhizosphere signaling, root turnover, and adaptive responses to environmental conditions.
Mycorrhizal fungi transfer plant-derived carbon into fungal biomass, hyphal networks, microbial communities, and stable soil carbon pools.
Microbial communities transform plant-derived carbon into microbial biomass, necromass, mineral-associated organic matter, and persistent soil carbon.
Soil aggregates physically protect organic matter from decomposition while promoting long-term carbon storage.
Water availability, infiltration, drainage, erosion control, and soil moisture strongly influence carbon retention and stabilization.
Regenerative management rebuilds soil carbon through living roots, cover crops, compost, biodiversity, reduced disturbance, grazing integration, and fungal networks.
Increasing soil carbon strengthens nutrient cycling, biodiversity, water retention, resilience, productivity, and ecosystem stability.
Stored soil carbon influences atmospheric carbon dioxide, soil respiration, greenhouse gas dynamics, and long-term climate regulation.

protectedRetrievalUse: soil carbon systems knowledge mesh retrieval
